mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses why modern technology developed in Europe rather than China despite earlier Chinese innovations including gunpowder, printing, and the compass, engaging general conceptions of technological development (A), the diffusion of Western technology (B.6), and the full arc of technology's history (C).

Pitt-Rivers organized artifacts typologically to argue that human technology evolved incrementally like biological organisms, directly engaging general conceptions and definitions of technology (A) and material culture of ancient technological development (C.1).

Smith's analysis of division of labor and wealth accumulation was composed amid early Industrial Revolution transformations, directly connecting technology and wealth (B.1) with the sociocultural consequences of industrialization (C.3).

Marx's analysis of capitalism's internal contradictions, labor exploitation, and surplus value directly engages the relationship between technology and wealth (B.1) and how industrial technology reshaped social institutions and class structures (B.5).
Industrialized killing, nuclear weapons, and the technological transformation of warfare directly address technology and war (B.2) and twentieth-century technological developments (C.4).

Covers harnessing nuclear energy for both power generation and weapons, directly addressing technology and war (B.2) and Space Age nuclear power technology (C.4.b), including moral consequences of atomic research.
Turner's painting symbolizes the transition from sail to industrial steam power, directly engaging technology and art (B.4) and advances in power technology during the Industrial Revolution (C.3.a).

Morris diagnosed industrial capitalism's destruction of meaningful craft labor and sought to reunite beauty with daily life through the Arts and Crafts movement, directly addressing technology and art (B.4) and technology's impact on social institutions (B.5).

Ruskin's central argument linked art and architecture to moral values, contrasting medieval craftsmanship with degraded industrial labor, engaging the relationship between technology and art (B.4) and technology's effects on social institutions (B.5).

Examines social, economic, and political consequences of the IT revolution including digital divides and media convergence, addressing technology and wealth (B.1), technology and social institutions (B.5), and Space Age computing and communications (C.4.b).

Examines how the Industrial Revolution transformed lifestyles, health, housing, and labor relations as workers moved to factory towns, directly addressing technology and social institutions (B.5) and sociocultural consequences of industrial change (C.3).

Analyzes the transition from industrial to information-based economies and declining social capital, directly addressing technology and social institutions (B.5) and late twentieth-century technological transformation (C.4.b).

Mechanization, automation, and digital technology progressively displaced traditional craftsmanship, directly addressing how technology reshaped institutions of labor (B.5) and the arc of twentieth-century technological change (C.4).

Enclosure displaced rural laborers into urban wage work, creating conditions enabling the Industrial Revolution, connecting technology and social institutions (B.5) with the sociocultural preconditions of industrialization (C.3).

Gaskell's novel explores class tensions and new social structures created by factory production in a manufacturing town, addressing technology and social institutions (B.5) and the development of textile industries (C.3.b).

The Jantar Mantar observatory represents Indian technology at the pivotal moment before European colonialism displaced traditional methods, addressing Western vs. non-Western technology (B.6) and the emergence of Western technology 1500-1750 (C.2.b).

Iron smelting technology spreading from the Middle East around 1000 BC produced harder tools enabling agricultural expansion through iron ploughs and new settlement patterns, spanning the beginnings of technology (C.1.a) and the urban revolution (C.1.b).

Traces urban civilization from eighth-millennium-BC Mesopotamia through Athens and Rome, covering how technology including aqueducts, roads, and infrastructure drove urbanization across the ancient world (C.1.a through C.1.c).

The collapse around 1200 BC destroyed interconnected palatial economies dependent on bronze metallurgy and long-distance trade networks, illustrating the urban revolution period's technological dependencies (C.1.b).

A first-century-BC bronze astronomical computer demonstrating Hellenistic precision metalworking and mechanical engineering unmatched for over a millennium, directly exemplifying technological achievements of Greece and Rome (C.1.c).

Traces writing technology from classical antiquity to the invention of printing, covering changes in materials and formats, directly relevant to medieval technological advances including the invention of printing (C.2.a).

The magnetic compass as medieval navigation technology (C.2.a) and Gilbert's De Magnete (1600) as systematic scientific study (C.2.b) connect natural philosophy with practical technological application across the medieval and early modern periods.

The spread of the printing press in seventeenth-century England expanded book production and democratized knowledge access, illustrating the emergence of Western technology in the 1500-1750 period (C.2.b).

Galileo's telescope, Kepler's optical theory, and the development of spectacles, telescopes, and microscopes exemplify the invention of early scientific instruments during the emergence of Western technology (C.2.b).

Boulton and Watt advanced steam engine technology while Priestley discovered oxygen and Wedgwood pioneered industrial manufacturing, bridging the emergence of Western technology (C.2.b) and Industrial Revolution power technology (C.3.a).

Covers mechanized textile production, Watt's steam engine, iron and steel manufacturing, and factory organization, directly addressing advances in power technology (C.3.a) and development of key industries (C.3.b).

Watt's steam engine improvements and Carnot's theoretical work on engine efficiency directly drove Industrial Revolution power technology, connecting scientific understanding of heat with advances in power technology (C.3.a).

Faraday's discovery of electromagnetic induction established the principle underlying electric generators, transformers, and power stations, advancing power technology (C.3.a) and enabling the electrical industry (C.3.b).

Brunel's bridges, tunnels, and railways represent cutting-edge Victorian engineering in transportation infrastructure, exemplifying the development of transportation industry during the Industrial Revolution (C.3.b).

Edison's telegraph improvements, phonograph, incandescent light bulb, and electrical distribution systems, plus the pioneering Menlo Park industrial research laboratory, bridge late Industrial Revolution industries (C.3.b) and early twentieth-century rationalization of production (C.4.a).

Staudinger's 1920s proof of polymer existence led to synthetic materials including nylon, plastics, paints, and adhesives, directly exemplifying early twentieth-century synthesizing of fibres and plastics (C.4.a).
